trade.summary: Japan and Mexico are linked by the Japan-Mexico Economic Partnership Agreement (JMEPA, 2005), one of Japan's earliest bilateral EPAs. Japanese auto manufacturers — Toyota, Honda, Nissan — have major manufacturing operations in Mexico. Trade is driven by automotive parts, electronics, and machinery. Mexico's large Japanese-owned manufacturing base (maquiladoras) processes goods for the North American market.

diplomatic.summary: Japan and Mexico enjoy warm diplomatic relations stretching back to the 1888 Treaty of Amity. Mexico hosts one of the largest Japanese diaspora communities in Latin America. Both countries cooperate in multilateral forums and share democratic values. Mexico is an important strategic partner for Japan in Latin America.

military.summary: No formal military alliance. Defense cooperation is minimal — Japan focuses its military partnerships on Asia-Pacific allies. Occasional disaster relief and humanitarian coordination exercises occur. Mexico's non-interventionist foreign policy limits military cooperation.

financial.summary: Japanese FDI in Mexico is substantial, primarily driven by the automotive sector (Toyota, Honda, Nissan, Mazda, Mitsubishi), electronics, and retail. Japanese companies operate major production facilities in Guanajuato, Aguascalientes, and the northern border states. Mexico is among the top recipients of Japanese FDI in Latin America.

energy.summary: Mexico exports some crude oil to Japan, though volumes are modest compared to Japan's Middle Eastern and Australian supplies. Growing interest in Mexican LNG and renewable energy projects.

scientific.summary: Scientific cooperation is growing, with joint research programs in disaster risk reduction, agriculture, and environmental science. The Japan International Cooperation Agency (JICA) runs multiple technical assistance programs in Mexico. Academic exchanges are modest but growing.
